# tokens: # user-defined type kinds — dispatch matrix
# Every refinement kind a user can introduce via `def Name (refine …)`
# or `def Name (… tor …)` etc. must dispatch the same way system types
# do: a typed fn parameter `[x:Name]` accepts values the type admits
# and rejects everything else.
# The earlier bug: `ResolveDefType` in eng/go/fn_params.go fell through
# to `return TAny` for any populated type body other than Record /
# Options — so `[f:Foo]` where Foo was a `refine Object` body became a
# wildcard. Compounded by missing per-kind Behavior.Match wiring for
# Disjunct / DepScalar / bare nominal subtypes (each minted a lattice
# node with the default lattice-walk Match, which rejected every value
# because the new node sat BELOW its parents in the lattice).
# The fix has two layers:
#   1. ResolveSigType now consults `DefEntry.TypeDef *Type` (the
#      explicit "this is a type" flag) via `r.LookupTypeName` as the
#      primary path; body-payload pattern-matching is the fallback for
#      Record / Options only.
#   2. InstallType (eng/go/core_type.go) attaches a per-kind Unifier
#      Behavior at mint time:
#        - Object / Table → parent-chain walk (default)
#        - Disjunct       → disjunctUnifier (unifies against alternatives)
#        - DepScalar      → depScalarUnifier (runs the bound check)
#        - bare refine X  → bareRefineUnifier (admits base-type values)
#        - predicate fn   → predicateUnifier (pre-existing)
# Rows below pin both directions per kind — accept the right values
# AND reject the wrong ones. A regression in either layer surfaces
# immediately.
# Runs against the production lang registry (test/go/langspec).
# 1. Object refinement — `def Foo (refine Object {…})`
# A typed fn param [f:Foo] should accept Foo instances built with
# `make Foo {…}` and reject unrelated values.
def Foo (refine Object {}) def g fn [[f:Foo] [Integer] [99]] (make Foo {}) g
def Foo (refine Object {x:0}) def g fn [[f:Foo] [Integer] [99]] (make Foo {x:5}) g
note: Foo with field, accepts instance carrying field

# tokens: def Foo (refine Object {}) def g fn [[f:Foo] [Integer] [99]] 42 g
def Foo (refine Object {}) def g fn [[f:Foo] [Integer] [99]] 'hi' g
def Foo (refine Object {}) def g fn [[f:Foo] [Integer] [99]] [1,2,3] g
# Regression for §6.1 — with Foo dispatching correctly, no need for
# `end` between `g` and the next native fn (`print`-equivalent). Before
# the fix this leaked the user-fn call into the next word because the
# wildcard match captured the next word as an argument.
def Foo (refine Object {}) def g fn [[f:Foo] [Integer] [99]] (make Foo {}) g lengthq [ 1 ]
# 2. Bare nominal subtype — `def Pos (refine Integer)`
# A bare refinement adds no structural constraint, so any value of the
# base type structurally satisfies the refinement.
def Pos (refine Integer) def g fn [[n:Pos] [Integer] [99]] 42 g
def Pos (refine Integer) def g fn [[n:Pos] [Integer] [99]] -5 g
def Pos (refine Integer) def g fn [[n:Pos] [Integer] [99]] 'hi' g
def Pos (refine Integer) def g fn [[n:Pos] [Integer] [99]] [1,2,3] g
def Name (refine String) def g fn [[s:Name] [Integer] [99]] 'Ada' g
def Name (refine String) def g fn [[s:Name] [Integer] [99]] 42 g
# 3. Disjunct refinement — `def Maybe (Integer tor none)`
# A disjunct admits values matching any alternative.
def Maybe (Integer tor none) def g fn [[m:Maybe] [Integer] [99]] 42 g
def Maybe (Integer tor none) def g fn [[m:Maybe] [Integer] [99]] none g
def Maybe (Integer tor none) def g fn [[m:Maybe] [Integer] [99]] 'hi' g
def Maybe (Integer tor none) def g fn [[m:Maybe] [Integer] [99]] [1,2] g
# Mixed alternatives.
def Mixed (Integer tor String) def g fn [[m:Mixed] [Integer] [99]] 7 g
def Mixed (Integer tor String) def g fn [[m:Mixed] [Integer] [99]] 'x' g
def Mixed (Integer tor String) def g fn [[m:Mixed] [Integer] [99]] true g
# 4. DepScalar refinement — `def Big (Integer gt 10)`
# A DepScalar attaches a constraint to a base scalar type. Dispatch
# must check (a) base-type compatibility AND (b) the constraint.
def Big (Integer gt 10) def g fn [[n:Big] [Integer] [99]] 100 g
def Big (Integer gt 10) def g fn [[n:Big] [Integer] [99]] 11 g
def Big (Integer gt 10) def g fn [[n:Big] [Integer] [99]] 10 g
def Big (Integer gt 10) def g fn [[n:Big] [Integer] [99]] 5 g
def Big (Integer gt 10) def g fn [[n:Big] [Integer] [99]] 'hi' g
# Lower-bounded refinement.
def Small (Integer lt 0) def g fn [[n:Small] [Integer] [99]] -5 g
def Small (Integer lt 0) def g fn [[n:Small] [Integer] [99]] 5 g
# 5. `is` vs dispatch — two DIFFERENT questions about types
# The `is` word and the fn-sig dispatcher answer DIFFERENT questions
# about a value/type relationship — by design, not by mistake.
#   - Dispatch asks: "can v be passed where T is expected?"
#     → loose binding semantics. A bare-refine subtype `Pos` of
#       Integer admits any Integer (no narrowing constraint added).
#     → see §2 above — `[n:Pos] g 42` returns 99.
#   - `is` asks: "does v carry T's tag?"
#     → strict identity semantics. `42 is Pos` is false because 42's
#       runtime type IS Integer, not Pos. To get a Pos-tagged value
#       use `Pos unify 42` (which produces Pos:42), or build via
#       a constructor.
# The asymmetry is intentional and is locked in by the pre-existing
# test typed_def_test.go::TestRefineBareDistinctFromAlias:
#   def Foo Integer        ; 42 is Foo  → true   (alias — Foo IS Integer)
#   def Bar refine Integer ; 42 is Bar  → false  (subtype — distinct)
# So the two questions agree for cases where there's no narrowing
# (builtin types, untagged values against alias types) and diverge
# for refinements that introduce a fresh nominal tag.
# Disjunct: an Integer 42 IS one of Maybe's alternatives. Tag of 42
# is Integer, which IS in Maybe's structural decomposition. The
# disjunctUnifier admits it.
def Maybe (Integer tor none) 42 is Maybe
note: Disjunct: Integer is one of the alternatives

# tokens: def Maybe (Integer tor none) 'hi' is Maybe
# DepScalar: the bound constraint, when run on the value's tag, must
# admit the value. unifyDepScalar checks the base type AND the bound.
def Big (Integer gt 10) 100 is Big
def Big (Integer gt 10) 5 is Big
note: DepScalar: tag is Integer but 5 not > 10

# tokens: # Bare refine: tag identity. An untagged 42 is NOT a Pos.
def Pos refine Integer 42 is Pos
note: bare refine: 42's tag is Integer, not Pos

# tokens: # Object refine: an instance constructed via `make Foo {}` carries
# the Foo tag. So `(make Foo {}) is Foo` is true.
def Foo refine Object {} (make Foo {}) is Foo
def Foo refine Object {} 42 is Foo
# Builtin: lattice subtyping. 42's tag is Integer; Integer ≤ Number ≤ Scalar.
42 is Integer
42 is Number
42 is String
# 5b. A typed-def binding TAGS the value, resolving the divergence.
#     `def y:Pos 2` does not merely check 2 against Pos — it binds y to
#     a value reparented to carry the Pos tag (see eng/go Typed-Def
#     Reparent). So `y is Pos` is true, unlike the untagged literal
#     `2 is Pos` (false, §5 above). The tag then widens up the lattice:
#     Pos ≤ Integer ≤ Number ≤ Scalar. An alias typed def against a
#     builtin (`x:Integer`) carries the builtin tag the same way.
def x:Integer 1 x is Integer
def x:Integer 1 x is Number
def x:Integer 1 x is Scalar
def Pos refine Integer def y:Pos 2 y is Pos
note: typed-def :Pos tags y — y is Pos

# tokens: def Pos refine Integer def y:Pos 2 y is Integer
def Pos refine Integer def y:Pos 2 y is Number
def Pos refine Integer def y:Pos 2 y is Scalar
def Pos refine Integer def y:Pos 2 2 is Pos
note: untagged literal 2 is not Pos (contrast with y)

# tokens: # 6. :Any is universal — Any means Any, including function values
# The :Any parameter type must NOT be narrowed by the user-subtype
# fix. Any wildcard semantics are intentional.
def f fn [[x:Any] [Integer] [42]] 5 f
def f fn [[x:Any] [Integer] [42]] 'hi' f
def f fn [[x:Any] [Integer] [42]] [1,2,3] f
def f fn [[x:Any] [Integer] [42]] none f
